natural_sources: Human and animal urine (main excretion product)
natural_occurrence.description: A nitrogen-containing compound that is the main end product of protein metabolism in mammals. Naturally present in urine. Synthetic urea is identical to natural urea and is used in chewing gum to prevent hardening and maintain texture.

manufacturing.description: Industrially produced by reacting ammonia with carbon dioxide at high temperature and pressure (Bosch-Meiser urea process). First organic compound to be synthesized from inorganic materials (1828).

eu.conditions: Authorized only for use in chewing gum without added sugar

usa.notes: Approved for use in chewing gum

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Emulsifying, Gelling, Stabilizing or Thickening Agents

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: Not specified (acceptable for use in chewing gum)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (no safety concern at permitted levels)

regulatory_history.event: Friedrich Wöhler first synthesizes urea - breakthrough in organic chemistry
JECFA evaluates urea for food use
EFSA re-evaluates urea as food additive in chewing gum

adi_context.source_note: No ADI established - urea is a natural metabolite produced by the human body daily (20-30g/day excreted in urine). Food additive use is minimal compared to endogenous production.

fun_facts: The main component of urine - your body produces 20-30g daily
First organic compound synthesized from inorganic materials (1828 - major scientific breakthrough)
In your chewing gum to keep it soft and prevent hardening
Main ingredient in fertilizer - 90% of synthetic urea goes to agriculture
Also used in diesel exhaust fluid (AdBlue) to reduce emissions
Your body naturally produces more urea in one day than you'd consume in years of chewing gum
